sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, LEFT, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 20, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 19 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Left square: rows 14-17, cols 14-17 (didn't move with UP or LEFT)
- Right square: rows 39-42, cols 49-52 (moved right by 5 cols with LEFT action)
Wait, LEFT moved the right square to the RIGHT? That's consistent with what I observed earlier - LEFT moves squares apart (left square goes left, right square goes right).
But the left square didn't move with LEFT - it's still at cols 14-17. That means it hit a wall on the left side.
So the squares are constrained by the container walls in all directions.
Current state:
- Left square: rows 14-17, cols 14-17 (top-left of left container)
- Right square: rows 39-42, cols 49-52 (middle-right of right container)
I need to figure out the goal. Let me think about this more systematically. The game has two movable squares in two containers. The goal is likely to move them to specific target positions.
Maybe the target positions are indicated by some visual cue that I'm missing. Let me look at the container structure more carefully to see if there are any special markers or patterns.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal is to move both squares to the same row, or to specific positions that create a symmetric pattern.
Let me try to explore all possible positions for both squares and see if any configuration triggers level completion.
First, let me map out the reachable positions for each square:
- Left square: can move UP/DOWN within left container, LEFT/RIGHT within left container
- Right square: can move UP/DOWN within right container, LEFT/RIGHT within right container
But wait, LEFT/RIGHT moves BOTH squares simultaneously. So I can't move them independently horizontally.
Let me think about this differently. The squares move as a pair horizontally (LEFT spreads them, RIGHT brings them together), but they move independently vertically (each stops at its own container wall).
Actually, I need to reconsider. Let me trace the history:
1. Start: left square at cols 19-22, right square at cols 39-42
2. After 4 LEFT: left square at cols 9-12, right square at cols 49-52
3. After 1 RIGHT: left square at cols 14-17, right square at cols 44-47
4. After 5 UP: left square at rows 24-27 (then 14-17 after 2 more UP), right square at rows 39-42
5. After 1 LEFT: left square at cols 14-17 (didn't move), right square at cols 49-52
So LEFT/RIGHT does move both squares, but they can hit walls independently.
Let me try to systematically explore positions. First, let me reset to a known position and then try different combinations.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to move both squares to the same row. Currently they're at rows 14-17 and 39-42. Let me try to move the left square DOWN to match the right square's row, or move the right square UP (but it's blocked).
Wait, the right square is blocked at row 39. Let me check if it can move DOWN and then UP again, or if there's a different path.
Let me try moving DOWN to see if the right square can access different areas.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me systematically explore the reachable positions
# Current: left at rows 14-17, cols 14-17; right at rows 39-42, cols 49-52

# First, let me move both squares DOWN to see the full vertical range
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After 6 DOWN:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  Sky blue: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Now try RIGHT to bring squares together
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter 4 RIGHT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  Sky blue: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
print("\nSPACE:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'), last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "No effect yet", "LEFT": "Moves squares apart horizontally by 5 cols", "RIGHT": "Moves squares together horizontally by 5 cols", "UP": "Moves squares up by 5 rows (constrained by walls)", "DOWN": "Moves squares down by 5 rows"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black_containers": "Container structures with compartments"}, "hud_map": {"top_border": "Timer bar", "bottom_border": "Timer bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1: Two sky blue squares in black containers. Squares move in 5-pixel steps, constrained by container walls. LEFT/RIGHT moves both horizontally, UP/DOWN moves both vertically (but each stops at its own wall).", "goal_model": "Need to find target configuration - possibly specific positions in containers.", "recent_findings": ["Left square can reach row 14, right square blocked at row 39 when going up", "Squares hit walls independently"], "open_questions": ["What is the target configuration?", "Can squares reach all compartments?"], "current_plan": "Explore all reachable positions systematically.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE at multiple positions - no change"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
